metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the alarm threshold, period, and evaluation_periods for `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` in the stuck-state AC (e.g. threshold ≥ 1800 s, period = 60 s, evaluation_periods = 3, treat_missing_data = 'breaching').
findings.finding: The stuck-state alarm in `monitoring_autolander.tf` has no threshold, period, or evaluation-periods specified. An executor cannot write the Terraform `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` resource without knowing what value of `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` (and over how many periods) triggers the alarm — 'defines a CloudWatch alarm on THAT metric' is insufficient to produce a runnable resource.
findings.location: AC: Stuck-state alarm — 'monitoring_autolander.tf defines a CloudWatch alarm on THAT metric'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the stuck-state AC: 'The alarm fires when `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` >= 1800 (30 min) for 3 consecutive 60-second periods, treat_missing_data = breaching; the unit test asserts these exact Terraform attribute values.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an end-to-end smoke-test AC: after `docker compose up`, `curl -sf http://localhost:<status-port>/autolander/status | jq -e '.heartbeat_age_s'` succeeds AND `curl -sf https://<gerrit-host>/autolander/status | jq -e '.heartbeat_age_s'` succeeds (or document a rationale for deferring the nginx integration check to a named child ticket).
findings.finding: The nginx routing change (`rebar.conf.template` new `location /autolander/status`) is a production code-path cutover with no end-to-end proving command. The Tests AC only covers a unit test of the status endpoint JSON shape — it does not exercise the compose-published port → nginx proxy_pass → bot response path as it will run in production.
findings.location: AC: Read-only status endpoint — nginx `location /autolander/status`; Files: `infra/compose/nginx/rebar.conf.template`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'e.g. :8080' with a decided port number (e.g. state 'listens on :8080; this is the canonical value used in compose, nginx proxy_pass, and land's status URL') so no executor choice is required.
findings.finding: The port value for the status endpoint is expressed as 'e.g. :8080', which is a placeholder pattern — an executor must choose the actual port, creating an undocumented decision that affects compose file, nginx proxy_pass, and `land`'s hardcoded URL.
findings.location: AC: Read-only status endpoint — 'listens on an in-container port (e.g. :8080)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change to: 'the endpoint listens on :8080 (decided value; compose publishes 8080, nginx proxy_pass http://autolander:8080, land polls https://<gerrit-host>/autolander/status).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the no-session_log constraint, e.g. `grep -rn 'session_log' infra/autolander/loop.py` returns zero matches, or a unit test asserts no file-open call targeting 'session_log' is made during a forced error cycle.
findings.finding: The 'service logs to container stdout and NEVER writes session_log' criterion has no stated proving command; it is a negative assertion with no in-session verification path.
findings.location: AC: 'The service logs to container stdout and NEVER writes session_log'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Tests AC: 'CI asserts `grep -c session_log infra/autolander/loop.py` == 0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the division of responsibility: state explicitly whether loop.py calls PutMetricData directly for `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` and `autolander_time_to_land_seconds`, or emits a stdout marker/value that observability.sh parses and forwards via PutMetricData — and align the Files descriptions accordingly.
findings.finding: Cross-section contradiction on metric emission responsibility: the Files section assigns `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` and `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` PutMetricData to `observability.sh`, while the `loop.py` file description also claims 'the time-to-land / time-in-phase metric emit' — an implementer cannot determine from the plan whether loop.py calls PutMetricData directly or only emits a marker/stdout value that observability.sh then reads and forwards.
findings.location: Files section (loop.py description vs. observability.sh description) and AC for stuck-state and time-to-land metrics
findings.suggested_fix: If observability.sh is the sole PutMetricData caller (consistent with the error-metric pattern), change loop.py's description to 'emits stdout metric lines (e.g. AUTOLANDER_METRIC_TIME_IN_PHASE <seconds>) that observability.sh reads' and remove PutMetricData language from loop.py. Alternatively, if loop.py calls PutMetricData directly for these metrics, remove them from observability.sh's description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the relevant docker-compose file (e.g. `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`) to the Files section as an EDIT, covering the port-publish for the bot's status endpoint.
findings.finding: The AC for the status endpoint states 'the compose service publishes it' (the in-container status port), but no docker-compose file is listed in the Files section — the AC implies a compose file edit that is not tracked as a deliverable.
findings.location: Files section (no compose file listed) vs. AC 'Read-only status endpoint' ('the compose service publishes it')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` (or the equivalent compose file) to Files with description 'EDIT: publish the autolander status port (e.g. 8080) to the compose network so nginx can proxy to it'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concurrency/isolation model for the status HTTP server (e.g. daemon thread with a short `timeout` on `handle_request`, `asyncio` with a read-deadline, or a separate process) so a stalled HTTP client cannot block the main polling loop.
findings.finding: The status endpoint's concurrency/isolation model is unspecified: if the HTTP server runs on the bot's main event loop or main thread, a slow or hung inbound HTTP request (e.g. a client that connects and stalls) could block the bot's core polling/pipeline logic.
findings.location: AC — 'Read-only status endpoint' / infra/autolander/loop.py
findings.suggested_fix: Run the status server in a `daemon=True` background thread using `http.server.HTTPServer` with a short `socket.timeout` (e.g. 2 s), or use a non-blocking asyncio server; call this out explicitly in the plan and in loop.py's design note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expose the stuck-state alarm threshold as a Terraform variable (e.g. `var.autolander_stuck_phase_threshold_seconds`) with a documented default and a comment explaining how to derive/tune it from observed p95 phase durations.
findings.finding: The CloudWatch alarm threshold for `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` (the stuck-state alarm) is not specified anywhere in the plan and is not marked as a Terraform variable — it will likely be hardcoded at an arbitrary value and is expected to evolve as operational data accumulates.
findings.location: AC — 'Stuck-state alarm' / infra/terraform/monitoring_autolander.tf
findings.suggested_fix: Add `variable "autolander_stuck_phase_threshold_seconds" { default = 900 }` in the tf file (or a shared variables file), reference it in the alarm resource, and note in the plan that the default should be revised after one sprint of production data.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the status-endpoint JSON schema (field names, types, semantics) and the marker-emission convention in a CLAUDE.md section or a lightweight ADR so that both sides of the contract are maintainable independently.
findings.finding: The heartbeat_age_s field is a hard, un-versioned cross-component contract between the autolander's status endpoint and land's lander_down logic; the plan introduces no schema documentation, no versioning strategy, and no update to CLAUDE.md/an ADR for this novel inter-service binding.
findings.location: AC — 'Read-only status endpoint' (heartbeat_age_s paragraph) / infra/autolander/loop.py ↔ land client
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Autolander observability contract` section to CLAUDE.md listing: (1) the status endpoint JSON shape with field semantics; (2) the marker line format (`TOKEN {JSON}`); (3) the Terraform alarm expressions that depend on them — one page is enough.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that observability.sh PutMetricData calls include a short timeout (`--cli-read-timeout 5`), log failures to stderr/stdout, and that a separate 'metrics pipeline healthy' check (e.g. CloudWatch's own metric `PutMetricData` error rate, or a missing-data alarm treatment of `breaching`) is in place.
findings.finding: The observability.sh PutMetricData calls have no error-handling, retry, or timeout specified; a transient AWS API failure will silently drop metrics with no fallback.
findings.location: infra/scripts/observability.sh — all three PutMetricData feeders
findings.suggested_fix: Set `TreatMissingData: breaching` on the autolander_errors alarm in monitoring_autolander.tf so that a silent metric-emission failure (data gap) triggers the alarm rather than masking it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify deploy ordering (autolander status endpoint live before `land` config update) and define `land`'s behavior (fail-open with a sentinel `heartbeat_age_s` or a timeout+retry) when the endpoint is unreachable during rollout or rollback.
findings.finding: Deploy ordering between the `land` client (which binds `lander_down` to `heartbeat_age_s` from the status endpoint) and the autolander service going live with that endpoint is unspecified; no coexistence behavior is defined for the window when the endpoint is absent.
findings.location: AC — Read-only status endpoint bullet: '`land` reaches it at `https://<gerrit-host>/autolander/status`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Deploy order' note: '1. Deploy autolander with status endpoint. 2. Verify endpoint responds. 3. Update `land` config.' Also specify that `land` treats a connection error as `heartbeat_age_s = ∞` (i.e., lander_down) rather than raising.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the heartbeat file path and creation event (e.g., 'loop.py writes it on first iteration'), and specify the `heartbeat_age_s` sentinel value (e.g., `-1` or `null`) returned when no heartbeat has been written yet — and ensure the test for `heartbeat_age_s` covers this absent-file case.
findings.finding: The heartbeat file's lifecycle is underspecified: who creates it, its filesystem path, and what `heartbeat_age_s` returns on first run before any heartbeat has been written are all absent from the plan.
findings.location: AC — Read-only status endpoint bullet: '`heartbeat_age_s` (integer seconds since the last heartbeat write)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC, add: 'If no heartbeat file exists, `heartbeat_age_s` is returned as `null` (or a large sentinel like `99999`). The loop writes the heartbeat on the very first successful poll iteration, so absence means the process has never completed a loop.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the HTTP handler safely reads `wipChain`, `phase`, `tested_shas`, and `heartbeat_age_s` as a consistent snapshot (e.g., a threading.Lock around the read, or publishing an immutable snapshot dict after each loop iteration that the handler reads atomically).
findings.finding: Concurrency between the HTTP status endpoint handler (reading `wipChain` and phase data) and the main autolander loop (updating that same in-memory state) is not addressed; no locking or snapshot strategy is specified.
findings.location: Files — `infra/autolander/loop.py`; AC — status endpoint serves 'current `wipChain` (change, `phase`, `tested_shas`, time-in-phase)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the loop.py spec: 'After each poll, the loop atomically replaces a module-level `_status_snapshot` dict (under a threading.Lock or by assignment of an immutable dict) that the HTTP handler reads. The handler never accesses mutable loop state directly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test that simulates a stale heartbeat (mock/advance the clock or write a past timestamp) and asserts `heartbeat_age_s` returns the correct elapsed seconds, covering the `lander_down` boundary condition (not just field presence).
findings.finding: The status endpoint test checks only that the JSON shape includes `heartbeat_age_s`, but never asserts its semantic correctness under the critical boundary condition: a stale heartbeat (large `heartbeat_age_s`) that causes `land`'s `lander_down` path to fire. This is a new user-facing flow (the endpoint is the surface `land` reads) tested with happy-path-only coverage.
findings.location: AC: Tests bullet — 'the status endpoint returns the documented JSON shape (incl. `heartbeat_age_s`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the unit test for the status endpoint, inject a mock clock or set the last-heartbeat timestamp to a known past value (e.g. 120 s ago), call GET /autolander/status, and assert `heartbeat_age_s >= 120`. Add a symmetric test with a fresh heartbeat asserting `heartbeat_age_s < 5`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK test to also assert that a metric datapoint is emitted for `autolander_errors` (matching the ERROR test's 'marker + a metric datapoint' pattern), covering the full HANDBACK → observability.sh → PutMetricData pipeline.
findings.finding: The test for `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` only asserts the marker is produced, but omits the metric-datapoint assertion that the parallel `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` test includes ('marker + a metric datapoint'). Since HANDBACK is one of the three feeders for `autolander_errors`, the observability.sh → CloudWatch pipeline for HANDBACK is untested.
findings.location: AC: Tests bullet — 'a forced hand-back produces an AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK marker' (no '+ a metric datapoint' unlike the ERROR case)
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the ERROR test structure: in the HANDBACK test, after asserting the marker line is written, also assert that the mock PutMetricData call receives a datapoint for the `autolander_errors` metric name (or that observability.sh, given a log line containing AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK, produces the correct PutMetricData invocation).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct `infra/autolander/loop.py` in the Files section from EDIT to NEW (and confirm whether `infra/autolander/` directory creation is in scope for this ticket or a prerequisite from S5a).
findings.finding: `infra/autolander/loop.py` is labeled EDIT in the Files section, asserting the file (and its parent directory `infra/autolander/`) already exist, but a directory listing of `infra/` shows no `autolander/` subdirectory at all — the file does not exist in the repo.
findings.location: Files section: `infra/autolander/loop.py — EDIT`
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Files entry to `infra/autolander/loop.py — NEW` and add a note confirming whether the `infra/autolander/` directory is expected to exist (created by S5a) or must be created here.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Files section nginx path from `infra/compose/nginx/rebar.conf.template` to `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` (the actual path confirmed in the repo).
findings.finding: The Files section names the nginx config path as `infra/compose/nginx/rebar.conf.template` but the actual file in the repo is at `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` — there is no `compose/nginx/` subdirectory in `infra/`.
findings.location: Files section: `infra/compose/nginx/rebar.conf.template (or the active nginx config)`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `infra/compose/nginx/rebar.conf.template (or the active nginx config)` with `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template — EDIT: add a read-only location /autolander/status proxying to the bot's status port.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Files section path from `infra/compose/nginx/rebar.conf.template` to `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` (the actual host nginx template; no `infra/compose/nginx/` directory exists).
findings.finding: The Files section names `infra/compose/nginx/rebar.conf.template` but the file lives at `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`. nginx is a HOST package (not a compose service) and its config template is in `infra/nginx/`, not `infra/compose/nginx/`. No `infra/compose/nginx/` directory exists.
findings.location: Files section: `infra/compose/nginx/rebar.conf.template (or the active nginx config)`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `infra/compose/nginx/rebar.conf.template` with `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` in the Files list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` to the Files list (EDIT: add the status-port `ports:` mapping to the `autolander` service) so the endpoint is reachable from the host nginx proxy.
findings.finding: `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` is an unenumerated required consumer: AC1 explicitly states 'the compose service publishes it' (the status port), which requires adding a new port mapping to the autolander service in docker-compose.yml, but docker-compose.yml is absent from the S5c Files list.
findings.location: AC1 (Read-only status endpoint): 'the compose service publishes it'; Files section omits docker-compose.yml

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either enumerate `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` (to emit the status port) and `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` (to expand the envsubst variable list) in the Files section, or explicitly specify that a hardcoded port is used in the nginx template so no new variable is needed.
findings.finding: The nginx template `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` is processed by `envsubst` using only `${REVIEW_BOT_PORT}` as the substitution variable (hardcoded in the template header and in `fetch-secrets.sh`). Adding a new `/autolander/status` proxy block requires either a new template variable `${AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT}` (which would require updating `fetch-secrets.sh` to emit it into `.env`) or a hardcoded port — but neither `fetch-secrets.sh` nor `compose-up.sh` are listed in S5c's Files despite being the envsubst driver and the nginx-reload caller respectively.
findings.location: Files section; AC1 network path; `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` lines 4–11

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Dockerfile for the autolander container (or add to an existing one) that declares a `HEALTHCHECK` instruction — the `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` feeder in `observability.sh` requires it; include the Dockerfile in the Files list.
findings.finding: The `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` feeder for the `unhealthy` container signal is silently inert unless a HEALTHCHECK instruction exists in the autolander container's Dockerfile. Without a HEALTHCHECK, Docker never populates `.State.Health.Status` (it returns an empty string, not `"unhealthy"`). The plan's Files list includes no Dockerfile for the autolander, and the existing Dockerfile.reviewbot has no HEALTHCHECK — confirming this is not automatic.
findings.location: AC: Error metric — feeder (3): 'the container `unhealthy` Docker health-state (via `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` (or the relevant Dockerfile) to the Files list and include a `HEALTHCHECK` stanza, e.g.: `HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --retries=3 CMD stat /path/to/heartbeat.json && test $(( $(date +%s) - $(stat -c %Y /path/to/heartbeat.json) )) -lt 90`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` to the Files list and specify the autolander service entry — including `ports:` (publishing the status port to host loopback), `logging: driver: journald`, and `healthcheck:` — so all three observability mechanisms have their required compose backing.
findings.finding: `docker-compose.yml` is absent from the Files list, yet the plan requires the autolander to be a new compose service. Without a service entry: (a) the status endpoint port cannot be published to the host so nginx can proxy to it; (b) no `logging: driver: journald` means `observability.sh`'s `journalctl CONTAINER_NAME=...` grep finds no container journal; (c) no `healthcheck:` means the `docker inspect` health-state feeder produces empty output. All three core mechanisms depend on compose configuration that is never specified.
findings.location: Files section — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` is not listed

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the stuck-state alarm parameters in the AC: threshold in seconds (what constitutes 'stuck' for `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds`), period (e.g. 300 s), and evaluation_periods — so the implementer can write a valid, intentional `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` without inventing operational parameters.
findings.finding: The stuck-state alarm AC says `monitoring_autolander.tf` defines a CloudWatch alarm on `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds`, but never specifies the threshold (seconds), evaluation period, or evaluation count. A `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` resource cannot be written without these parameters, and 'stuck' is operationally undefined without a threshold — the implementer must invent a critical operational number with no guidance.
findings.location: AC: Stuck-state alarm — 'monitoring_autolander.tf defines a CloudWatch alarm on THAT metric'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence to the status-endpoint AC (or Why section) explaining why the HTTP server is embedded in `loop.py` rather than a separate sidecar — e.g., 'runs in a background thread so it reflects live loop state without an IPC layer, acceptable because the endpoint is read-only and cannot block the loop'.
findings.finding: The plan gives no positive rationale for embedding the status HTTP server inside `loop.py` (the bot's own process) rather than alternatives such as a lightweight sidecar process or reusing the existing review-bot's FastAPI app. The 'Why' section explains the feature goals but not the design choice for where the server lives, leaving the implementer without isolation-boundary guidance.
findings.location: AC: Read-only status endpoint — 'a small read-only HTTP endpoint on the bot'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The server runs in a daemon thread inside loop.py so it reads loop state directly without IPC; it is intentionally thin (read-only, no auth, no body parsing) so a stuck HTTP client cannot block the main loop thread.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit authentication statement for `GET /autolander/status`: either name the mechanism (e.g. 'nginx restricts this location to the same GitHub OAuth session Gerrit enforces' or 'protected by an `auth_request` directive') OR provide a justified no-auth rationale (e.g. 'data is equivalent in sensitivity to Gerrit's public change list, which is already unauthenticated; the endpoint discloses no credentials or internal IPs') — the plan's current 'non-secret' characterisation is not a security rationale.
findings.finding: The plan stands up GET /autolander/status as an internet-reachable endpoint (via nginx at https://<gerrit-host>/autolander/status) but neither specifies a human/admin authentication mechanism nor provides an explicit, justified no-auth rationale.
findings.location: AC: 'Read-only status endpoint' — 'nginx routes GET /autolander/status … so land reaches it at https://<gerrit-host>/autolander/status. Read-only, non-secret (queue state, no credentials).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The `/autolander/status` nginx location requires no authentication because its data (change number, phase, queue depth, heartbeat age) is equivalent in sensitivity to Gerrit's public REST `/changes/` endpoint already served unauthenticated on this host; no credentials, tokens, or host-internal details are exposed. If this posture changes (e.g. change numbers become sensitive), add an `auth_request /gerrit_auth` directive mirroring the Gerrit session check.' This makes the no-auth decision explicit and reviewable rather than an omission.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly require in the plan (and in monitoring_autolander.tf) that both the `autolander_errors` alarm and the `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` stuck-state alarm wire `alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]` (and optionally `ok_actions`), matching the monitoring_88ab.tf / monitoring_ws7.tf pattern rather than the silent monitoring_s4b.tf pattern.
findings.finding: The plan specifies alarms in monitoring_autolander.tf but does not state that alarm_actions will wire the existing aws_sns_topic.alerts SNS topic, leaving open the possibility of silent (actionless) alarms — a gap the repo already exhibits in monitoring_s4b.tf.
findings.location: AC: 'Error metric (LOUD by default)' — 'Alarm in monitoring_autolander.tf'; AC: 'Stuck-state alarm (high visibility)' — 'monitoring_autolander.tf defines a CloudWatch alarm on THAT metric'.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Files' or AC section: 'monitoring_autolander.tf: both alarms set `alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]` (the shared SNS topic declared in monitoring.tf); ok_actions likewise so recovery is also signalled.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `(e.g. :8080)` example in the status-endpoint AC with a port that is not already bound by Gerrit (e.g. `:8081` or a named `${AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT}` variable), and confirm the chosen port is threaded consistently through docker-compose.yml, nginx location block, and fetch-secrets.sh.
findings.finding: The plan's concrete example port for the autolander status endpoint (`:8080`) is already occupied by Gerrit on the host loopback (`127.0.0.1:8080:8080` in docker-compose.yml). Any attempt to publish the autolander's status port as 8080 on the host will fail with a port-binding conflict at container start.
findings.location: AC — Read-only status endpoint: 'the endpoint listens on an in-container port (e.g. :8080)'
findings.suggested_fix: Pick a distinct port (e.g. 8081) and name it `AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT`; publish it as `127.0.0.1:8081:8081` in docker-compose.yml, proxy `/autolander/status` to `http://127.0.0.1:${AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT}/status` in the nginx template, and emit it from fetch-secrets.sh alongside `REVIEW_BOT_PORT`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) move `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` and `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` emission to a HOST-side section in `observability.sh` (reading values from the status endpoint JSON via `curl`), matching the existing pattern, OR (b) add `http_put_response_hop_limit = 2` to the `metadata_options` block in `main.tf` and document the security trade-off, and confirm boto3 credential acquisition is tested in the unit-test environment.
findings.finding: The Files section assigns `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` and `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` PutMetricData calls to `loop.py` (in-container Python/boto3), but the host's IMDS hop limit is 1 (no `http_put_response_hop_limit` in `main.tf`). The existing codebase explicitly avoids in-container AWS calls for exactly this reason: observability.sh line 118 reads 'Greping journald on the HOST avoids giving the container AWS creds (the IMDS hop limit constrains in-container metadata access).' Boto3 inside the autolander container cannot reach the instance metadata to obtain credentials, so PutMetricData calls from `loop.py` will silently fail with a credentials error at runtime.
findings.location: Files section — `infra/autolander/loop.py` — EDIT: 'the time-to-land / time-in-phase metric emit'; AC — Time-to-land metric and Stuck-state alarm ACs

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the script that renders `rebar.conf.template` via `envsubst` and update its variable-list argument to include `${AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT}` (e.g. `envsubst '${REVIEW_BOT_PORT} ${AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT}'`), or use a hardcoded port in the template to avoid the envsubst scoping issue entirely.
findings.finding: The nginx template (`rebar.conf.template`) is processed by a scoped `envsubst '${REVIEW_BOT_PORT}'` invocation (documented in the template's own header). Adding a new `${AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT}` variable to the template for the `/autolander/status` location block without also updating the `envsubst` variable-list will cause the literal token `${AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT}` to remain in the rendered `/etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf`, producing an nginx config-test failure (`nginx -t`) and preventing the nginx reload.
findings.location: Files section — `infra/compose/nginx/rebar.conf.template` — EDIT; AC — Read-only status endpoint, 'nginx routes GET /autolander/status … to it'
findings.suggested_fix: If a hardcoded port is used (e.g. 8081), write it directly into the nginx location block rather than as a template variable, eliminating the envsubst scope issue. If a variable is preferred, find the `envsubst` call site and extend the quoted list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the autolander status port compose publish binding as loopback-only (`127.0.0.1:<host-port>:<container-port>`) in the plan — matching the existing review-bot and Gerrit HTTP binding patterns — so the endpoint is reachable only via nginx TLS.
findings.finding: The autolander status endpoint's compose port-publish binding is not constrained to loopback (127.0.0.1), leaving open the possibility it is published to 0.0.0.0 and directly internet-reachable on the host's public IP — bypassing nginx TLS and making the endpoint accessible in plaintext on the host port.
findings.location: AC: Read-only status endpoint — 'the compose service publishes it'
findings.suggested_fix: In the compose service definition for autolander (planned in S5a), specify `ports: - "127.0.0.1:${AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT:-8081}:<container-port>"` (mirroring the review-bot pattern at line 97 of docker-compose.yml), and reference this constraint explicitly in the S5c status-endpoint AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare the `/autolander/status` endpoint's access class in the plan — e.g. 'intentionally public/unauthenticated because data is non-sensitive operational state and `land` needs no credential to read it' — so the posture is a conscious documented choice rather than an omission.
findings.finding: The status endpoint's access posture is never formally declared: the plan says 'read-only, non-secret' but does not state whether the endpoint is intentionally public/unauthenticated-with-rationale or intended to be internal-only. Per the rubric, an undeclared access posture on a boundary-crossing path is itself the finding.
findings.location: AC: Read-only status endpoint — 'Read-only, non-secret (queue state, no credentials)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete `phase` enum vocabulary (all valid values including `idle` and every non-idle state) in AC1 or a dedicated 'Shared contracts' section so the status-endpoint schema, time-in-phase publisher, and alarm expression all bind to the same set.
findings.finding: The `phase` field in the status-endpoint JSON is referenced as an enum with at least one named sentinel value (`idle`) in AC3, but the complete vocabulary of valid phase values is never defined anywhere in S5c. The stuck-state alarm fires only on non-`idle` phases, so an executor cannot write the `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` emission logic, the alarm threshold logic, or validate the endpoint's JSON shape without knowing the full set.
findings.location: AC1 ('the current `wipChain` (change, `phase`, `tested_shas`, time-in-phase)'), AC3 ('emitted each poll while non-`idle`')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet under AC1: "**`phase` values (complete set):** `idle` | `rebasing` | `awaiting_verified` | `submitting` (add any others from S2's loop states)." Reference this from AC3 and the test AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'stack hand-back' precisely in AC2: specify the exact loop.py code path (e.g. exception caught at the top-level iteration, current wipChain returned to the queue, loop continues) that triggers `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK`, and provide a concrete example of when it differs from an `AUTOLANDER_ERROR`.
findings.finding: The `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` marker is referenced as a distinct feeder for `autolander_errors` (AC2), but the concept of a 'stack hand-back' is never defined — neither what event triggers it, how it differs operationally from an `AUTOLANDER_ERROR`, nor what the JSON payload must contain. Every other existing marker in the repo (`VOTER_ERROR`, `AUTODEPLOY_ERROR`, `MERGE_CHANGE_ERROR`) has an explicit trigger condition co-located with its definition.
findings.location: AC2 — Error metric feeder (2): 'an `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` marker (the loop writes it on every stack hand-back — since a hand-back, not the client-side `lander_down`, is what the bot itself can emit)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC2 (or a 'Shared contracts' section) the exact log-source command pattern for the AUTOLANDER_ERROR/HANDBACK feeders in `observability.sh`, specifying the container name env var (e.g. `AUTOLANDER_CONTAINER`) and confirming it follows the `journalctl CONTAINER_NAME=` pattern used by all existing feeders.
findings.finding: The `observability.sh` feeders for `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` and `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` are specified to 'grep the leading token' (AC2), but the plan never specifies the source command — journalctl with a container name, `docker logs`, or otherwise — nor the container name to use. All existing feeders in `observability.sh` use `journalctl CONTAINER_NAME=<name>` with an env-var-controlled container name; the autolander container name is never stated in S5c.
findings.location: AC2 — '`observability.sh` greps the leading token to count occurrences'; Files section — `infra/scripts/observability.sh` EDIT
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: "**Log-source (parser side):** `observability.sh` reads the autolander container's journald via `journalctl CONTAINER_NAME="$AUTOLANDER_CONTAINER" --no-pager -o cat` (same pattern as `VOTER_CONTAINER`); the env var defaults to `compose-autolander-1` (matching the compose service name from S5a)."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` PutMetricData ownership conflict: state unambiguously in AC3 whether `loop.py` emits a stdout marker that `observability.sh` reads-and-publishes (consistent with the existing VOTER_ERROR pattern and IMDS hop-limit), or whether `loop.py` calls `PutMetricData` directly (requires in-container AWS creds), and remove the contradicting owner from the Files section.
findings.finding: The `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` metric's `PutMetricData` caller is ambiguous: the Files section assigns `loop.py` responsibility for 'the time-in-phase metric emit' AND assigns `observability.sh` responsibility for the '`autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` `PutMetricData` feeders', while AC3 states 'the loop publishes a `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` metric (`PutMetricData`)'. This creates two candidate owners of the AWS API call with no authoritative resolution.
findings.location: Files section (loop.py: 'time-in-phase metric emit'; observability.sh: '`autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` `PutMetricData` feeders'), AC3 ('the loop publishes a `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` metric (`PutMetricData`)')

findings.finding: The CloudWatch alarm threshold for the stuck-state alarm on `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` is never stated. An executor cannot write the `monitoring_autolander.tf` alarm resource without a concrete threshold value (e.g. 'alarm when metric > 900 seconds for 2 consecutive periods'). This blocks implementation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Stuck-state alarm bullet; infra/terraform/monitoring_autolander.tf
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the stuck-state AC: 'Alarm when `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` exceeds 900 seconds (15 minutes) for 2 consecutive 60-second evaluation periods; treat missing data as not-breaching.'

findings.finding: The plan labels `infra/autolander/loop.py` as EDIT and the AC asserts it already emits `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` on unhandled errors, `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` on hand-backs, and already tracks `wipChain`/`phase`/`tested_shas`/time-in-phase — but the directory `infra/autolander/` does not exist anywhere in the repository. Every AC bullet that says 'the loop writes X' or 'the loop publishes Y' rests on a file that has yet to be created by a sibling ticket.
findings.location: Files section — `infra/autolander/loop.py` (labeled EDIT); AC: Error metric, Stuck-state alarm, Status endpoint, Tests
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dependency on the sibling ticket(s) that create loop.py (e.g. S2a / 89f9-b381-60e2-4802). Restate the AC to say 'the loop MUST emit AUTOLANDER_ERROR on any unhandled error' (a requirement) rather than 'the loop writes it' (a false existence claim).

findings.finding: The Files section assigns `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` as an observability.sh PutMetricData feeder, while the AC and Tests AC assign emission of that same metric to loop.py (Python PutMetricData call). This cross-section contradiction sends the implementer in two directions about which component owns the emission.
findings.location: Files section — `infra/scripts/observability.sh` description; Time-to-land AC; Tests AC
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` from the observability.sh Files description. The loop.py entry already says 'the time-to-land metric emit', the AC says the loop emits individual Value datapoints, and the Tests AC verifies a Python PutMetricData call — observability.sh should only handle the three error feeders for `autolander_errors`.

findings.finding: The Files section for `infra/scripts/observability.sh` claims it adds a `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` `PutMetricData` feeder, but the plan body (Time-to-land AC) specifies that `loop.py` emits this metric directly as individual `Value` datapoints per landed change — observability.sh is the periodic host-side probe, not a per-event metric emitter. There is no mechanism for observability.sh (a 5-minute cron-style timer) to relay per-event `time_to_land` datapoints; this is a scope mismatch in the Files section.
findings.location: Files section: `infra/scripts/observability.sh` — 'EDIT: the autolander_errors + autolander_time_to_land_seconds PutMetricData feeders'
findings.suggested_fix: Files section for `loop.py`: 'EDIT: the read-only HTTP status endpoint + the `AUTOLANDER_ERROR`/`AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` structured stdout markers + `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` `PutMetricData` emit (per landed change) + `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` `PutMetricData` emit (each poll while non-idle)'. Files section for `observability.sh`: 'EDIT: the `autolander_errors` `PutMetricData` feeders (AUTOLANDER_ERROR stdout grep, AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK stdout grep, docker-inspect unhealthy state)'.

findings.finding: The stuck-state alarm on `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` does not specify a `treat_missing_data` policy, and the plan's chosen mechanism breaks in both failure directions: if set to `notBreaching`, a bot that freezes BEFORE the per-poll metric emit (a total hang with no output) is invisible — the alarm sees missing data as healthy; if set to `breaching`, a legitimately idle bot (no active wipChain, metric correctly not emitted) generates a perpetual false alarm. The plan says the metric is 'emitted each poll while non-idle' but does not describe how the alarm distinguishes 'idle' from 'frozen', leaving the stuck-state detection goal unachievable without resolving this policy choice.
findings.location: AC: Stuck-state alarm (high visibility) — `monitoring_autolander.tf` CloudWatch alarm definition
findings.suggested_fix: Have the loop emit `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds = 0` each poll even when idle (a heartbeat-style zero), so the metric is always published while the bot is alive. The alarm can then use `treat_missing_data = breaching` (missing metric = bot is frozen/dead) and threshold `> N seconds` to catch stuck non-idle phases, while the zero datapoints during idle keep it from alarming on a healthy idle bot.

findings.finding: The plan declares the autolander alarms should be 'LOUD by default' but does not specify that monitoring_autolander.tf wires alarm_actions to the shared SNS alerts topic — the closest structural analog (monitoring_s5.tf) has no alarm_actions and is therefore a silent alarm.
findings.location: Files: infra/terraform/monitoring_autolander.tf — NEW; AC: 'Alarm in monitoring_autolander.tf'; AC: 'Error metric (LOUD by default)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC or Files description: 'monitoring_autolander.tf: all alarms set alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn] and ok_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn] (the shared SNS topic from monitoring.tf), consistent with monitoring_autodeploy.tf; the silent-alarm pattern from monitoring_s5.tf must NOT be used here.'

findings.finding: The plan assigns `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` publication to BOTH `loop.py` (Files section: 'the time-to-land metric emit') AND `observability.sh` (AC bullet: 'observability.sh publishes `autolander_errors` + `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` `PutMetricData` feeders'). These are mechanically incompatible: `observability.sh` is a periodic host probe that reconstructs counts from cumulative journald offsets, but `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` must be a per-event latency value emitted when a specific change lands — there is no offset-delta mechanism that can reproduce a duration. The viable integration path (loop.py direct boto3 emit, matching voter.py) is obscured by the simultaneous claim that observability.sh also publishes it.
findings.location: Files section ('the time-to-land metric emit') vs AC: Time-to-land metric ('observability.sh publishes … autolander_time_to_land_seconds')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section keep 'time-to-land metric emit' under loop.py. In the AC, change 'observability.sh publishes autolander_errors + autolander_time_to_land_seconds' to 'observability.sh publishes autolander_errors; loop.py emits autolander_time_to_land_seconds directly via boto3 (matching the voter.py pattern) on each landing event.'

findings.finding: The decision rule 'emit `autolander_time_in_phase_seconds` each poll while non-`idle`' does not address `phase=paused` (the emergency-stop state). Since `paused` is a valid non-`idle` phase per S2b's enum and represents a legitimate operator-initiated pause (not a stuck bot), including it in metric emission will cause the stuck-state alarm in `monitoring_autolander.tf` to fire as a false positive whenever an operator pauses the bot. The plan provides no fallback or exclusion rule for this case.
findings.location: AC: Stuck-state alarm (high visibility) — 'emitted each poll while non-`idle`'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the stuck-state AC to: 'emitted each poll while `phase ∉ {idle, paused}` (the `paused` emergency-stop state is deliberate and should not trigger the stuck-state alarm).'

findings.finding: The per-event `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` metric has a split-publish architecture that risks data loss: loop.py is designated the emit site but observability.sh is designated the PutMetricData feeder. A time-to-land value is produced exactly once per landed change (an instantaneous event), not at a fixed cadence. If observability.sh runs on a timer (e.g. every 5 minutes like the other probes), any landed change whose emit occurs between two probe runs can only be recovered if observability.sh parses loop.py's stdout for those events — but the plan never specifies this log-scraping mechanism, an offset file, or any bridging contract between loop.py and observability.sh for this per-event value.
findings.location: AC: Time-to-land metric / Files: infra/autolander/loop.py + infra/scripts/observability.sh
findings.suggested_fix: Have loop.py call PutMetricData directly (via boto3 or subprocess aws CLI) immediately when a change lands, with Value = elapsed seconds. Remove observability.sh's role for this metric. This is consistent with how the loop already has direct knowledge of when a change lands and matches the 'in-session proof = the datapoint is published' AC.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
